41 - 5 Tonalities To Master To Close Any Deal - YouTube.mp4
Did you know that tonality is 10 times more important than the words you use if you want to close more deals? Make sure you watch what I'm about to show you right here because there's five types of tonalities that you have to master if you want to make a lot more sales than you are now, if you want to become a top 1% salesperson for what you sell. The first type of tonality. This is called a curious tone. Now, why do I say that you need to master a curious tone? Because prospects— range recognizes patterns, right? And what is a pattern that they might recognize from a salesperson that says cold calls them or some type of telemarketer, something like this monotone voice. Hey, is this John?
Hey, John, it's Jeremy Miller with XYZ Company. Hey, the reason why I'm calling you is, and that monotone tonality is a pain. Pattern they recognize. Oh, salesperson, telemarketer, guard goes up. Does that make sense? Okay. If I change my tone into a curious tone, that implies that I'm genuinely curious about what they're doing, or I'm genuinely curious about a question I'm asking. So let's give you another example. Let's say, for example, beginning of a conversation. And let's say, if I sold for a marketing agency. Okay. So, okay. So it looks like you booked on the calendar about, I guess, maybe getting higher. Qualified leads to really grow the business, right? That's a curious tone. It sounds like I'm genuinely curious.
Or I might say, 'So what were you hoping could, I guess, possibly happen by working with us?' See, that's a curious tone. It implies that you're curious, okay? That's the number one. Number two, this is called the confused tone. Now, why do I say a confused tone? I don't mean confused tone that you're confused. I don't know how the thing works. I don't know how the product works. Or if they ask a question, you're confused. I'm not really sure about the answer. A viewer's tone can be used in different contexts. So let's say, if you want them to emotionally open up to you more, and they say the X, Y, Z problem is causing them a lot of stress. Oh, X, Y, Z problem. It's just causing so much stress in our department.
Hold on. How do you mean by stress? Or stress? See, my facial expression and my tone imply I'm not understanding. I'm confused what you mean by stress. Now, what does the prospect do? Subconsciously, their brain immediately says, 'Oh, he didn't understand what I meant by stress.' I need to explain that better. I need to expand on that. And suddenly they're like, 'Oh. Gosh.' And they start just emotionally opening up. Oh gosh, you have no idea the stress. And they start telling you what they mean by stress. Okay. That is one way that confused tone does that. In that context. But if I didn't use it confused, I'm like, 'Oh, what did you mean by stress?' Well, it's just sometimes get stressful.
You're not going to get the same thing because you've got to get them to like expand on it in a curious tone in that context wouldn't do that, but a confused tone would, Oh, how, how do you mean by stress? Okay. It sounds a little bit concerned to open some up. Motioning, okay? Number three, the third type of tone you have to master is a challenging tone, okay? Now, not challenging, you're not going to use a challenging tone in the first couple minutes of a conversation. But let's say you're three-fourths of the way in. You've built a big gap from where they are to where they want to be. They feel urgency. They feel pain. They have a fear of future pain, okay, and you've helped him see what the future will look like, future pacing.
Once that pain is gone, I want to rip that away with the challenging tone. I might say, 'So what happens if you don't do anything about this and this actually gets worse?' See, I'm challenging them. But if I did that too early in the conversation, they would get defensive. Now I have trust and credibility by this point and I can challenge them. I can even use a softer tone, but a challenging tone. What if this doesn't change? Okay, so I'm challenging them, but I'm using a softer tone to do that. Okay, challenging tone. Okay, there's a lot more to that for sure. The fourth type of tonality you have to master, this is one of the most important things, is the concern tone. Okay. The tone that shows empathy.
Now, why do I say you need to have a tone that shows empathy? You're not going to ask every question with a concerned tone. That would be awkward. Okay. But let's say, if, let's say, if you sold life insurance, for example, and the person I might ask a question like, 'So is something recently happened or what's caused you to feel like you might need? More coverage.' Well, yes, we've had a death in the family. Oh, what actually happened? Yeah, my grandma died. Oh, wow, you seem so young. What actually happened to your mom? Well, my mom did. And did she like, what type of insurance did, did she leave with your dad? Okay. I'm concerned. It's a concerned tone. Okay. Or I might move in if, let's say, they're, they're not moving forward.
They're not opening up. And I might say, can I ask you something, John? Sure, go ahead. And this is just between you and I, you know, off the record. What's really holding you back? See that soft concern tone. It's a tone that shows empathy. And when you show empathy in those types of contexts, there's a lot more that builds massive trust. They feel like you genuinely care for them. If you don't show empathy in some of those contexts, if you just use like a monotone tone or a curious tone, they don't feel like you care about them. They just feel like you're trying to sell them. And remember, if you've heard me say this, is selling something you do to people or is selling something you do for people?
It's for people because if we don't master selling, they don't buy from us and their problems don't get solved and they don't get where they want, all because we have it. You have to really master is called a playful tone.
Okay. Now, when I say playful tone, we don't use a playful tone to be like the class clown or the... crack jokes. That's not what I'm talking about. A playful tone is used strategically throughout the conversation when certain contexts come up and that's to help. the prospect let their guard down. So let's say you get on it. Let's say you have a call. Let's say you call a prospect or whatever. And within like 30 seconds, they actually ask you how you're doing. They're like, 'Hey, well, how are you doing now? And what would most salespeople say? Oh, I'm doing really good. Or I'm just working hard, which that doesn't really hurt you, but it doesn't really help you. OK, so if the prospect said, 'Well, hey, how's how's your day gone?
Oh, you know, just trying to stay out of trouble over here. What about you? Are you getting any trouble? Oh, no, no, I'm good. See, now they start. To laugh a little bit. Guard comes down. Now, why do they do that? Because scientifically, that releases what's called dopamine in their brain, which is actually causing them. To let their guard down.' Say just a way to get their guard down. Relax the nervous system. You get the nervous system to relax, they become open. They're not guarded. Is it easier to sell when the prospect is not guarded compared to when the wall of resistance is up? You obviously know the answer. Now there are subcategories of each of these tones. So a curious tone, there's also a subcategory called, there's many, but I'll just list a few here, called a familiar tone.
Okay. So let's say if you're a cold caller, or you call outbound leads instead of sounding like everybody else does. Remember your prospects recognize patterns in your vocal cords from other salespeople that say. A monotone, or way too excited, or too timid, and in somebody that's nervous, okay— you want to sound like the expert, calm, collective, assertive. You're the expert who can solve their problems, but you're not overbearing. Okay. Now, familiar tongue, instead of saying 'on a cold call,' is this John? Hey John, it's Jeremy Miller with XYZ company. Hey, the reason why I'm calling you was, click. I want them to feel like my voice knows them, like I'm familiar with them. So immediately, I'm not going to say, 'is this John?' I'm going to say, 'John.' Yeah, hey, it's Jeremy.
Jeremy Miller with XYZ Company. You responded to the ad. You asked us to call you back about looking at the 2026 Blue Audi, right? Let's see. It was an outbound lead. I just made that up. Okay. See what I'm doing there. I'm speaking into the future, but that sounds, yeah, it's Jeremy, Jeremy Miner. You asked us to call you back. voice sounds similar, but it's how I cadence it. It's how I pace that out. It's Jeremy, Jeremy Miner with XYZ Company. You asked us to call you back. You saw the ad this morning. Facebook about looking at, like, different properties for sale in XYZ area. I sold real estate. If I ran an ad, see. It doesn't matter what the industry is. It's Jeremy. Jeremy Minor. See that's that familiar tone.
Like if you ever got a call from somebody like, 'Hey, it's Amy, Amy Smith.' How are you? What do you do? Yeah, I'm doing good. How are you? You. And then the whole time your brain is thinking, 'What, who's Amy Smith? How do I know Amy Smith? She sounds familiar.' And all that's doing is buying time because the tone interrupted your pattern. Because it's a familiar tone that you feel like, 'Hey, this person knows me.' You're not going to be rude to that in the beginning. Does that make sense? Okay. So there's many different tones of these. I'll just show you. Few others here. Let's say a challenging tone. There's a subcategory, which is a skeptical tone.
Now, you don't want to be skeptical where you sound like you're judging them, but I might ask what's called an APQ consequence question. I'll be like, 'Oh.' So... How? How long have they had you actually do that for? See, I'm skeptical, but that implies that something could be wrong. I'm seeding doubt that there might be a better way. Oh, so they're still making you do that manually? What's caused them not to actually automate that so you don't have to spend four hours a day doing that manually? See, I'm not being skeptical towards them. I'm being skeptical towards what they're being forced to do. Let's say, if I sold some type of SaaS that automated something that they had to do manually, they would take them hours a day.
See, that's a skeptical tone. Now you want to master tonality for your industry.
